title: Cat Thickened and Brittle Nails: Causes, Triage, and Care
meta_desc: Learn why your cat has thickened or brittle nails. Discover common causes, why it is usually a low-urgency issue, and how photos help your vet.
content_html:
What Is It?

Thickened or brittle nails in cats can manifest as claws that look unusually wide, scaly, or prone to splintering. This condition can be caused by various factors, including the natural aging process, where cats may stop scratching as frequently to shed their nail sheaths. Other potential causes include fungal infections, nail bed inflammation, or systemic issues like hyperthyroidism or cutaneous horn growth.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for thickened or brittle nails is generally Low. While these changes require a veterinary evaluation to rule out underlying health issues, they are rarely life-threatening. However, if your cat is limping, if the nail has grown into the paw pad, or if there is active bleeding or pus, you should schedule an appointment sooner rather than later.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of the affected paw can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. High-quality images allow a professional to see the texture of the nail, the condition of the surrounding skin, and whether multiple claws are involved. This information helps them determine if the issue is a localized nail problem or a sign of a broader dermatological or metabolic condition, potentially saving time during your visit.

faqs.question: Is it normal for senior cats to have thicker nails?
faqs.answer: Yes, as cats age, they often become less active and may not use scratching posts as often. This leads to a buildup of nail sheaths, making the claws appear very thick and blunt.

faqs.question: Can I trim thickened nails at home?
faqs.answer: You can, but proceed with caution. Thickened nails can be more prone to splitting, and the 'quick' (the blood vessel) may be harder to see. Use sharp, high-quality clippers and only take off the very tip.

faqs.question: Could this be a sign of a fungal infection?
faqs.answer: Yes, fungal infections can cause nails to become brittle, discolored, or misshapen. A vet can perform a skin scraping or culture to confirm if fungus is the culprit.
